name: Cagn
description: Cagn, a central figure in the mythology of the San people of Southern Africa, holds a complex and multifaceted role. He is often considered a creator deity, though his creation is not ex nihilo. Rather, he is a transformer and arranger of an already existing world. Cagn is not depicted as an all-powerful, omniscient being; instead, he is frequently portrayed as a trickster, a shape-shifter, and a somewhat flawed character. His actions are often driven by curiosity, hunger, or a desire for amusement, leading to both beneficial and detrimental consequences for humanity. He is associated with the mantis, which serves as one of his primary animal manifestations, embodying his cunning and adaptability. Despite his trickster nature, Cagn is also seen as a benevolent figure who brought essential elements to humankind, such as fire and the knowledge of hunting techniques. He embodies the inherent contradictions and complexities of existence, reflecting the San worldview that embraces both the sacred and the profane within the divine.
subtitle: The Mantis Creator

keyMyths.title: Cagn and the Eland
keyMyths.description: The eland, a large antelope, holds significant ritual importance for the San. In one myth, Cagn creates the first eland, endowing it with special powers and connections to the spirit world. This act establishes the eland as a sacred animal and a vital link between humans and the divine. The myth details the hunting rituals associated with the eland, emphasizing the respect and reverence due to the animal, and the consequences of disrespecting Cagn's creation. This story underscores the importance of maintaining balance and harmony within the natural world.
keyMyths.source: Bleek, W. H. I., & Lloyd, L. (1911). Specimens of Bushman Folklore.

keyMyths.title: Cagn Steals Fire
keyMyths.description: Fire, a crucial element for survival, is often attributed to Cagn's actions. In this myth, Cagn obtains fire through trickery, often stealing it from a powerful being or animal who hoarded it for themselves. The details vary across different San groups, but the central theme remains the same: Cagn's cunning and resourcefulness bring fire to humanity, enabling them to cook food, provide warmth, and ward off predators. This act is a testament to his role as a benefactor, despite his often mischievous behavior.
keyMyths.source: Hewitt, R. L. (1986). Structure, Meaning and Ritual in the Narratives of the Southern San.

keyMyths.title: Cagn and the Origin of Death
keyMyths.description: While Cagn is often associated with creation and life, some myths also connect him to the introduction of death into the world. In one version, Cagn attempts to prevent death by instructing people to throw a shoe into the sky. If the shoe lands upright, life will continue; if it lands upside down, death will come. The shoe lands upside down, resulting in the introduction of mortality. This myth highlights the limitations of even a creator deity and the acceptance of death as an inevitable part of the cycle of life.
keyMyths.source: Barnard, A. (2007). Anthropology and the Bushman.
